One of the great semi-secret features in Basecamp is the ability to add multiple instances of a specific tool to a project page. We've elevated this feature in Basecamp 5 (launching soon).

For example, here's our "Mobile: BC5" project in Basecamp. This is where the mobile team is working on the Basecamp 5 apps (iOS, Android, and Desktop apps).

In this project they have a variety of tools (a message board for announcements and focused discussion, a chat room for this that and the other, a dedicated files area to keep assets/images/logos, etc, a couple of short to-do lists, etc), but they also have five different Card Tables (our version of kanban).

One is for mobile bugs in general, one's for desktop bugs, ones for tracking progress on Android, another for progress on iOS, and then they have another one for some specific big picture design stuff.

I can't stress enough just how incredible it is to work this way. Everything together here on a single project page. Yet specifics are compartmentalized so you can dive deep into different oceans, all from the same boat.

And as an outsider to this project, I can drop in when I'm curious and get up to speed quickly on everything related to the Mobile team's work on BC5 apps. And a single click on the Activity feed aggregates everything that happened on a single page, no matter where it happened, in the BC5 Mobile project. One place. One product. Nothing in my way, nowhere else I need to look to piece it together.

It's just an exceptional way to work. In the 27 years I've been running this company, and the 22 years we've had with Basecamp, I've never felt so on top of things, so informed, so involved, and so clear as I have using Basecamp 5. It's a huge level up.

Launching later this month.

Will drastically -- Will 'drastically underestimates the impact of fairy courts. You got that from A Subpoena for The Devil, right? Yeah, I read that too. 

Were you gonna plagiarize the whole thing for us? Do you have any thoughts of your own on this matter? 

Have you even read the Manidis Arabic canon? Do you even know how to play the al-Luʿbat al-Hāl (اللعبة الحال)? Or do you...is that your thing?

dwarkesh_sp.text:
Ancient people never decided to adopt farming.

Agricultural practices gradually emerged out of a compounding series of small decisions, and they spread less by choice than by cultural competition.

This major transition in human history was decided by nobody. https://t.co/uT4AOlHDPo

There are so many counterintuitive facts in human genetics, talking to David Reich was just surprise after surprise.

"There's many places in your DNA where you're more closely related to a Neanderthal on your mother's side than you are to your father." https://t.co/PB93RSAlZd

David Reich calls it "an outstanding mystery of human history":

All of the genetic ingredients that are present in modern humans have been there for ~50,000 years, maybe even longer. So why did it take us so long to develop farming? https://t.co/KJUZjjOX6b

Between 100,000 and 50,000 years ago, human culture and technology exploded.

You might expect that this points to a major step-change in human evolution.

But David Reich and colleagues looked at the genetic data, and found no significant sweeps in this period at all. https://t.co/MPPQ2yxWAD

David Reich doesn't think population size has been a big constraint on human evolution. Why?

(1) The human genome already contains more than enough diversity for natural selection - we generally don't need to worry about having a large enough population for new mutations.

(2) Sometimes if selection is weak enough, it can be swamped by random genetic drift in small populations. But then it would be so weak that, even in a large population, it wouldn't make a difference on the timescales of human history. So population doesn't really matter either way.

Hunter-gatherers faced food instability on a short time scale: they would gorge themselves after a hunt, then might go days without any meat.

So they have an evolutionary advantage to keeping a higher body fat percentage - it keeps them going after a hunt.

Agricultural societies don't have this kind of day-to-day food insecurity. Farmers worry about food shortages on the scale of years, with disease and famine; marginally higher body fat wouldn't help with that.

So when a population switches to agriculture, natural selection begins to operate in the opposite direction, reducing body fat.

There's a common idea that, as human society grew more complex, individuals could specialise and outsource much of their thinking to the culture at large.

On this view, intelligence should have been maxed out in the hunter-gatherer period. Once complex societies took over, selection for individual intelligence would weaken.

But David Reich's lab finds the opposite pattern in the genome. The period when selection most aggressively favoured intelligence-linked genes was the Bronze Age, right as humans were organising themselves into city-states and empires.

What was the most important transition in human history, the thing that most drastically altered our species' way of life? David Reich's lab has found evidence pointing to a new answer.

There are two standard candidates:
1. The shift from hunting and gathering to farming, around 10,000 BC.
2. The Industrial Revolution, around 1800 AD.

Here's one way to adjudicate this:

When a species' environment changes drastically, natural selection accelerates, because the species has to catch up to adapt.

So the biggest transition will have happened in the period with the most rapid natural selection.

What David's lab has found is that the period with fastest natural selection wasn't 12000 years ago, and it wasn't our modern period.

It was the Bronze Age, about 5,000 to 2,000 years ago.

What was it about the Bronze Age that changed humans so profoundly?

David Reich is back.

He and collaborator Ali Akbari just published a paper that overturns a long-standing consensus about human evolution — that natural selection has been dormant in our species since the agricultural revolution.

By scaling ancient DNA sequencing and developing a new statistical method, they found that selection has actually sped up.

Selection went especially bonkers during the Bronze Age (around 3,000 years ago).

That's when gene frequencies for everything from immune function to body fat to intelligence were most in flux.

Over the last 10,000 years, selection pushed the genetic predictor of cognitive performance up by roughly a full standard deviation — most of it between 4,000 and 2,000 years ago.

After we finished recording, David sketched out on a whiteboard his new heretical model about who the Neanderthals really were. Luckily, I took out my iPhone and managed to record it.

He thinks the standard story (that Neanderthals are some separate archaic lineage we interbred with a little) just doesn't fit the evidence. Instead, he proposes that Neanderthals are essentially genetically-swamped modern humans.

A small population somewhere around the Caucasus invented Middle Stone Age technology roughly 300,000 years ago and expanded outward. The ones that moved into Europe interbred with local archaic humans, got genetically swamped, and became Neanderthals. The same expansion went into Africa, met much more diverged archaic Africans, and that mixture became us.

This means Neanderthals and modern humans share the same cultural ancestry — the only difference is which archaic humans they mixed with afterward.

David is a brilliant and rigorous scholar. It was a real delight to learn from him again.

0:00:00 – Ancient DNA suggests strong selection over last 10,000 years
0:16:24 – Natural selection intensified during the Bronze Age
0:35:40 – Why didn't evolution max out intelligence?
0:58:00 – Evolution is limited by time, not population size
1:09:40 – Why no farming before the Ice Age?
1:17:52 – The Neanderthal puzzle David can’t stop thinking about
1:54:40 – The methodology behind this breakthrough

Even if the future goes extremely well, even if we manage to preserve and promote human values, that future could still be incomprehensible to us - because among our values is a belief in the importance of change and moral progress. @jkcarlsmith https://t.co/NhCrpwMJ4z

It's very interesting that cryptographic protocols and neural networks have the same high-level architecture (where they jumble information as it moves sequentially across many layers). 

This is the result of a convergent evolution - cryptographic protocols need every output bit to depend on every input bit in complicated ways, and similarly, NNs need output to make connections between inputs.

But they're in some sense doing opposite things. While cryptographic protocols take something which has a lot of structure and make it seem indistinguishable from random, NNs take something which may look random and extract structure from it.

Much more on this idea in the full episode with @reinerpope

About 5000 years ago, horse nomads from the steppes conquered Europe. Still today, almost every European language descends from the one they spoke, and as much as 90% of European DNA comes from them.

What did this conquest actually look like on the ground? https://t.co/V1Rf1STChv

Pre-industrial populations weren't just shorter and less healthy - they were less intelligent, too.

A combination of fewer calories and a much higher burden of infectious disease meant that people's brains just developed less during childhood. https://t.co/vliWZwVcyV

AI training faces a very different set of trade-offs compared to human evolution. Because you can directly copy a trained model basically for free, it makes sense to amortize in the pre-trained weights the learning that humans spread across a lifetime. https://t.co/xV6zV8AxoA

RT @samweinstein: “I could have been a pretty good cartoonist, or good at business, but the fact I was a cartoonist, good at business made…

My biggest takeaways from @EricRies:

1. There’s a force that pulls organizations toward mediocrity and corruption—“the force no one controls but everyone obeys.” It’s not greed or aging—it’s “financial gravity”: structural pressure from governance. Eric describes how a friend could literally taste when a restaurant had been taken over by private equity. The same pattern erodes brands, removes founders, and turns products into extractive machines.

2. Only 20% of venture-backed founders are still CEO three years post-IPO. Follow standard VC and legal advice, and you have an 80% chance of being pushed out. Everyone thinks they’re the exception—but statistically, you’re not.

3. Most corporate charters say companies exist to pursue “any lawful activity,” which effectively means maximizing shareholder value. This shareholder primacy hasn’t always been how it works. It has only dominated for about 40 years. Today, boards are legally obligated to take the highest offer—even harmful ones. Eric points out how Philip Morris acquired Vectura, destroying it within three years.

4. The key question isn’t what protections you need—it’s when. At every stage, founders are told “not yet”: lawyers say wait for PMF, Series A VCs say later, growth VCs say it’ll hurt fundraising, bankers at IPO say bundle it later, then the CFO at S-1 says, “Sorry, too late.” It’s never the right time—which means you have to do it early.

5. The simplest protection: become a “public benefit corporation.” This is different from a B Corp, and is used by companies like @AnthropicAI, with essentially no downside. It’s a short legal filing where you define your actual purpose. It doesn’t guarantee good behavior, but it protects you if investors push you to act against your mission.

6. Anthropic shows how a mission-aligned team and strong governance allow you to move faster. They allow independent AI safety experts—without financial incentives—to influence decisions. This enables Anthropic to refuse risky model releases or lucrative deals.

7. “Harder is easier.” @Cloudflare gave SSL away for free, even though it was their top conversion driver. Conversion dropped briefly, top-of-funnel grew 10x, and they became a $70B company. Principled decisions always lose on ROI spreadsheets in the short run—that’s why structural protection matters more than values statements.

8. Three things founders should do now: (1) become a public benefit corporation, (2) add a director’s oath tied to mission, and (3) implement mission-protective provisions (e.g. founder control, board votes). Optionally, reserve equity for a nonprofit foundation. These are easiest early, before investor constraints kick in.

Full conversation: https://t.co/FVRuUQGb2a
